venus_capricorn__moon_capricorn__air_dom.closing: Follow the physical truth of the material to find enduring structure + softness + one living texture.

style_core.text:
You dress for substance. Your primary sense is not sight, but weight. Trust your hands when you shop. Pick up a garment and feel its density, its quiet heft. Looks can be deceiving. Weight cannot. Your ultimate compass is the wrist test. Drape the fabric over your wrist, where the skin is thin. Close your eyes. Does it feel solid, dependable, real? Trust this physical instinct as your ultimate compass.

Your core formula is enduring structure + softness + one living texture. Picture a tailored coat in a heavy wool, its clean lines creating a frame. Underneath, the give of a fine-gauge knit against your skin. The final element is a pair of leather boots or a textured bag. Build your wardrobe deliberately. Each piece is chosen for the long term, judged not by trend but by its physical integrity. Apply the wrist test to every potential acquisition. If it feels flimsy or insubstantial, leave it behind. It is not for you.
style_core.sectionIntro: This is the blueprint for your style, the unchanging formula that guides every choice.
tests: trust your gut and walk away
the wrist test

textures_good.text: Your formula of enduring structure, softness, and one living texture is built from materials with integrity. You must learn to identify them by touch, not by sight. A material should yield to your grasp, not push back or whine when rubbed together. Your sense of substance is a reliable guide. Look for the warm, quiet give of {texture_good_1}, the reassuring weight of {texture_good_2}, and the living grain of {texture_good_3}. Feel how {texture_good_4} moves a fraction of a second after you do. These materials provide the essential softness and structure your formula demands. They wear in, not out. The following textures are ranked by their ability to deliver this quality. Commit them to your sense memory.
textures_good.sectionIntro: Master these materials to build a wardrobe with integrity.

textures_bad.text: Certain materials will lie to you. They may look right on the hanger, but they fail in your hand. {texture_bad_1} feels inert and thin, with no memory or body to it. It collapses when you squeeze it. {texture_bad_2} is worse, it generates a kind of static friction against your skin and feels slick in a dead way. {texture_bad_3} often has a hollow, papery quality that makes a rustling sound. Your hands will recognise these hollow promises. They offer no warmth, no pleasing drape, and no character that deepens with time.
textures_bad.sectionIntro: These materials fail the most important examination, the one your own hands conduct.
tests: the touch test
if a fabric resists your touch or feels squeaky between your fingers, leave it behind

textures_sweet_spot.text: Your ideal pairing combines the integrity of {sweet_spot_keyword_1} with the warm fluidity of {sweet_spot_keyword_2}. This is your formula in practice. A garment with a solid shape, like a heavy coat in {texture_good_1}, finds its purpose when paired with the grace of pure softness. You already know what works by feel. Trust the sensation of a material yielding to your grasp, rather than pushing back or whining when rubbed together. This interplay of a strong line and a gentle drape is where you will always look correct.
textures_sweet_spot.sectionIntro: Your signature lies in the conversation between surfaces.
tests: the touch test
if a fabric resists your touch or feels squeaky between your fingers, leave it behind

palette_narrative.text: Your palette is foundationally warm. Build your base from colours with inherent weight and depth. Use {core_colour_1} and {core_colour_2} to provide the enduring structure for your coats, trousers, and leather goods. Think of rich toffee wool or camel cashmere. {core_colour_3} and {core_colour_4} are your bridging tones. Use them in knitwear and linings. A taupe or a stone grey can ground a look without weighing it down. For shirting and silk blouses, use soft cream instead of stark white. Your accents are deliberate, never loud. Choose one deep, saturated colour to carry through your accessories. {accent_colour_1} and {accent_colour_2} are your choices here. Look for an oxblood belt, a burgundy leather portfolio, or a single plum silk scarf. These are points of focus, not distractions. Weave in earthy greens like olive and moss green for quiet complexity. Use them in tweed jackets or fine-gauge knits to introduce a natural element. Use misty blue-green sparingly. It is a point of cool relief, reserved for a single piece when the rest of your outfit is deeply warm and tonal. Pass over icy electric blue, neon brights, and any fleeting trend pastels. These colours lack the substance you require. Avoid the trap of head-to-toe black. It reads like you gave up on the way out the door. Break it up with a camel layer, a deep wine scarf, or a rich cognac belt to restore intention.
palette_narrative.sectionIntro: Master your colours to build looks with deliberate intention.

occasions_work.text: Your work attire is led by enduring structure. Look to a sharp, brushed wool blazer in camel or trousers in a heavy tweed that fall without a break. This is the framework. Softness is delivered through materials that move with you, not against you. A silk blouse in a warm cream, a fine-gauge cashmere knit. These prevent the composition from becoming rigid. The goal is presence, not performance. Remember the rule. A full day of meetings does not require collapsing your silhouette. Your one living texture grounds the whole. The subtle grain of a rich toffee leather belt, the solid weight of a briefcase in full-grain hide. Pick one. Let it be the only point of focus. Jewellery is minimal. A muted gold watch, simple silver studs. The two metals coexist quietly, they do not compete for attention.
occasions_work.sectionIntro: At work, your clothes perform a function. They must support your competence without distraction.

paying a utility bill does not require collapsing your silhouette (Blaze)

occasions_intimate.text: For evenings, the yielding quality of your garments takes precedence. Look for materials that absorb warm light, a silk top in rich toffee or a fine-gauge camel cashmere knit that drapes from the collarbone. The aim is not to be seen from across a room, but to be discovered up close. Your foundation of enduring structure always remains. A tailored wool trouser grounds a fluid top. A well-cut full-grain leather skirt anchors a delicate knit. Jewellery becomes a quiet detail, a matt gold chain resting on the clavicle or a simple silver cuff. Remember the pharmacy line. Paying a utility bill does not require collapsing your silhouette. An intimate dinner, however, invites it. This is the place for controlled drape and deliberate shape.
occasions_intimate.sectionIntro: In close quarters and low light, shift your focus to materials that reward proximity.
tests: the pharmacy line: a trip to the pharmacy does not require suffering for the aesthetic (Slate)
paying a utility bill does not require collapsing your silhouette (Blaze)

occasions_daily.text: Your standard of dress does not pause for errands. Casual activity is not an excuse to abandon form. For active days, look for materials that move with you, not against you. Choose a relaxed cashmere jumper in a warm, rich toffee. Pair it with trousers in brushed wool that hold their line from hip to ankle. Your trainers should be full-grain leather, kept clean, and the bag you carry must have its own form. The equation is constant. Enduring structure + softness + one living texture. A good knit has a pleasing density, a substantial feel. This is your guide.
occasions_daily.sectionIntro: Movement demands materials that breathe and shapes that follow your lead.

hardware_metals.text: Your metal strategy is one of two registers. Treat personal jewellery separately from structural hardware. For pieces worn next to your skin, stick to warm, muted metals. Look for {personal_metal_1} and {personal_metal_2} with a low lustre. For structural elements like zips, bag clasps, and belt buckles, your range expands to include {structural_metal_1} and {structural_metal_2}. This distinction gives you flexibility without disrupting your core warmth. Avoid the trap of high-shine finishes. Polished surfaces, particularly cool metals like {excluded_finish}, look sharp and distracting next to your muted colour palette. They create a discordant note. Stick to matt and brushed textures to keep the visual field cohesive. The object is not to draw the eye with a flash of light, but to build a complete texture story where every element belongs.
hardware_metals.sectionIntro: Here is how you will handle metal. Treat it as a structural element, not mere decoration.

hardware_stones.text: Your stones must feel solid, an anchor for the softness of a cashmere knit or a silk shirt. They are the dense counterpoint in your composition, chosen for their physical substance. Look for stones that hold light rather than reflecting it. They should have a deep, warm glow. Consider the saturated colour of {stone_1} or the cloudy depths of {stone_2}. A smooth cabochon of {stone_3} often has more visual weight than a brilliant-cut diamond. The goal is a quiet, steady presence, not a distracting flash. The settings must integrate, not fight against your clothes. A flash of polished chrome silver next to rich toffee wool looks jarring. Keep all settings for your stones in matt and brushed textures.

Your hardware requires a satisfying heft to serve as an anchor for your softer materials. A chain necklace should pour into your hand like liquid {metal_1}. A brooch set with {stone_1} must feel solid, not hollow. This physical presence provides the enduring structure your clothing needs.

accessory_1.text: Accessories are how you deploy the final term in your style formula. They provide the one living texture that settles against your look’s enduring structure and softness. Your approach here must be disciplined. Limit yourself to one or two strong pieces per look. Define the focal point. If an earring is doing too much, remove it. This is the keepsake test. If an item does not have weight or significance, it dilutes the effect. Begin with your bags. Look for full-grain leather that will gain character over time. The hardware, from buckles to clasps, should be brushed and matt. The purpose of these pieces is to provide a solid, textural anchor. Pick up a bag. Feel its construction. A well-made strap or handle feels substantial in your hand.

The fabric should feel substantial when you handle it, not flimsy. Drape it simply and let it settle without a fussy arrangement. A quality piece finds its own place. When you wear a scarf, it becomes the focal point for that area. Do not wear a necklace or distracting earrings that compete with it.

Avoid anything that glints or announces itself before the rest of the outfit. The same restraint applies to your jewellery. Wear small, personal pieces. An aged silver ring or an oxidised chain has a quiet history. Before you leave, check your work. If an earring and a necklace are both making a statement, one is doing too much. Choose one.
